behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithmic property test. The heuristic is a deterministic local function (coordinate list → route). Failure would be detected by feeding real test cases with known optimal solutions (from precomputation or brute force on small N) and measuring route quality. No external I/O, no mocking of core algorithm needed. We only need to verify the algorithm's output meets the quality bound.
behaviors.test_description: Generate test coordinate sets (5–20 stops) with precomputed or brute-force-verified optimal routes. Run the TSP heuristic. Measure the actual route length and verify it is ≤ 1.5× the optimal solution. Include edge cases: collinear points, clusters, random distributions.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This tests a pure mathematical formula: time = distance / speed. The function takes distance and speed as inputs and returns time. No external I/O needed if speed-by-road-type is a local configuration lookup. Failure would manifest as incorrect arithmetic or wrong speed selection. A Unit test directly provides inputs and verifies the formula.
behaviors.test_description: Create test cases with known distances and road types. Look up configured speed for each road type. Call the time estimation function with (distance, speed). Verify output equals distance / speed. Include edge cases: zero distance, unusual speeds, speed lookup misses.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Pure comparison and subtraction logic. Inputs: total route time, remaining shift hours. Output: reject/accept status and excess amount. No I/O, no external dependencies. Failure would be: incorrect comparison (e.g., uses > instead of ≥) or wrong excess calculation. A Unit test directly calls the validator and checks the result.
behaviors.test_description: Create test cases: route within limit, route exceeding limit, route exactly at limit, negative excess. Call validator with (total_time, remaining_hours). Verify correct rejection status and accurate excess calculation (total_time - remaining_hours).

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans multiple modules: input handling → geocoding (with failure handling) → skip logic → TSP → reporting. A Unit test of just the 'skip' logic wouldn't catch integration failures like: error not propagating, optimizer including the failed address, or report omitting the skipped item. MOCK-MISS: a mock that always succeeds hides the failure path. REAL-INFRA: while we can mock the geocoding API, the test must wire together the full pipeline to verify correct behavior across module boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Create an address list with one address that will fail geocoding. Mock the geocoding API to return empty results for that specific address. Call the service with this list. Verify: (1) the failed address does not appear in the optimized route, (2) remaining addresses are included and TSP-optimized, (3) the failure report lists the skipped address by name/coordinates.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Consolidation logic depends on geocoding to identify duplicates (same geocoded coordinates = same stop). While a Unit test with pre-geocoded data could test consolidation in isolation, the real failure is detected when realistic addresses with identical geocodes aren't consolidated in the service pipeline. MOCK-MISS: a mock geocoding always returning distinct coordinates would hide the consolidation failure. Testing at Integration level (mocked geocoding returning same coordinates for two addresses) verifies the consolidation works correctly in context.
behaviors.test_description: Create two distinct address strings that will geocode to the same coordinates. Mock the geocoding API to return identical coordinates for both. Feed the list to the service. Verify: (1) only one stop appears in the optimized route (not two), (2) delivery items from both input addresses are combined into a single stop, (3) the route doesn't revisit the same location.
